Luke opens his gospel not with theology but with testimony. He tells us that he has investigated everything carefully, traced it from the beginning, and now writes an orderly account so that you may know the certainty of the things you have been taught. This is not speculation or legend. Luke is claiming to have done the hard work of verification. He wants his reader, Theophilus, to understand that what follows is grounded in eyewitness testimony and careful inquiry. When you read the rest of his gospel, you are reading the work of someone who cared deeply about accuracy, who took seriously the weight of recording what God has done. That matters as you encounter the supernatural claims that follow—they come from a writer who values truth.

Into this carefully researched account, Luke places Zechariah and Elizabeth. Notice what he tells us about them first: they are righteous, walking blamelessly in all the commandments and decrees of the Lord. These are not peripheral characters. They are faithful, obedient people who have lived their entire lives in covenant with God. And yet they are childless. Their faithfulness has not earned them the one thing they desperately longed for. Then an angel appears—Gabriel himself, who stands in the presence of God—and announces that their prayer has been heard. Their son will be filled with the Holy Spirit from his mother's womb and will turn many back to the Lord. But when Zechariah asks how he can be certain, his doubt is met with silence. He will not speak until the child is born. The angel's rebuke is swift and severe: you did not believe my words, which will be fulfilled at their proper time. The certainty of God's word does not depend on our faith. It will be fulfilled. But Zechariah's silence reminds us that faith is not optional—doubt has consequences.

Then Gabriel is sent to a virgin named Mary in Nazareth. She too is troubled, and she too asks a question: "How can this be?" But her question emerges from a different place than Zechariah's. She does not ask "how can I be sure?" She asks "how is this possible?" And when the angel explains that the Holy Spirit will overshadow her, that the Holy One to be born will be called the Son of God, Mary responds with a submission that stands in stark contrast to Zechariah's doubt: "I am the Lord's servant. May it happen to me according to your word." Her words carry the weight of complete relinquishment. She knows nothing of what lies ahead. She will be misunderstood, scandalized, heartbroken. Yet she says yes. Elizabeth, filled with the Holy Spirit when Mary arrives, recognizes what is happening: blessed is she who has believed that the Lord's word to her will be fulfilled. And Mary's response—the Magnificat—reveals the staggering nature of what God is doing. He is exalting the humble and bringing down the proud. He is remembering mercy to Israel. He is fulfilling promises made to Abraham generations ago. This is not a private miracle for one young woman. This is the hinge of history. God is moving, and all of it hinges on the obedience of a girl from Nazareth who said yes when she had every reason to say no.
